coverage_limits: The conclusion concerns asymptotic comparison count under the supplied exact, sequential, arbitrary-key, no-preprocessing model.
No claim is made about constant factors, auxiliary space, stability, parallelism, approximate sorting, preprocessing, or non-comparison key models.
The pinned skill and theory battery define seven lens families; P8 and P9 therefore remain explicitly unmapped.

rigor.trigger: The fixture asks whether an algorithmic optimality claim is justified by a formal lower bound.
rigor.tier_reason: A theorem-level comparison is required, but the supplied closed mathematical model has no safety-critical or empirical premise requiring high-assurance treatment.

decision_frame.question: Is an O(n log n) exact sequential comparison sort asymptotically optimal for arbitrary keys when preprocessing is forbidden?
decision_frame.system_boundary: Comparison count as a function of n within the frozen sequential comparison model supplied by scenario.md and artifacts/facts.json.
alternatives: an O(n log n) comparison sort
a hypothetical o(n log n) exact comparison sort
authorized_objectives: establish or refute c1 using the applicable formal lower bound
decision_frame.priority_rule: Exact correctness for every allowed input precedes asymptotic comparison minimization.
assumptions: n is the number of keys
The stated O(n log n) bound is a worst-case comparison-count upper bound.
Distinct-key inputs may be used for the lower bound because they are a permitted subset of arbitrary-key inputs.
Each comparison of distinct keys has two possible outcomes.
decision_frame.uncertainty_posture: Use only the supplied frozen model and theorem-level deductions; do not extrapolate to excluded computational models.

coverage.reason: No relation, dependency, key, decomposition, or normalization choice is present.
No concurrent schedule, transaction isolation level, or recoverability property is involved.
No replication, partition, quorum, or distributed-consistency guarantee is involved.
The claim is specifically an asymptotic optimality assertion requiring matching O and Ω bounds in parameter n.
No implementation-level type, invariant-preservation, totality, or refinement choice must be assessed.
Exact sorting must distinguish n! possible total orders while each binary comparison supplies at most one bit of branching information.
No architectural coupling, duplicated source of truth, materialized view, or failure-domain decision is present.
The supplied pinned skill and theory battery define no eighth lens family.
The supplied pinned skill and theory battery define no ninth lens family.

derivations.model: A decision tree for exact sequential comparison sorting of n distinct arbitrary keys; internal nodes are binary comparisons and leaves identify output permutations.
preconditions: The algorithm is correct for every permitted input.
Only sequential key comparisons reveal relative order.
fact_mapping: artifacts/facts.json sets model to sequential comparison.
steps: Restrict the permitted arbitrary-key inputs to the subset containing n distinct keys; a lower bound on this subset is a lower bound for the full model.
There are n! possible input orderings, and exact sorting must distinguish all of them, so the decision tree requires at least n! leaves.
On distinct keys each comparison has two outcomes, so a binary decision tree of worst-case height h has at most 2^h leaves.
For n >= 2, n! >= (n/2)^(n/2), hence log2(n!) >= (n/2)log2(n/2) = Ω(n log n). Also n! <= n^n, so log2(n!) = O(n log n). Thus log2(n!) = Θ(n log n).
Consequently every exact sequential comparison sorter has worst-case comparison complexity Ω(n log n) under the frozen model.
derivations.result: The comparison model has a worst-case lower bound of Ω(n log n) comparisons.
residual_mismatch: This lower bound does not apply after leaving the comparison model, such as bounded-integer radix sorting.

derivations.construct: Matching upper and lower bounds with optimization convergence
derivations.model: Worst-case comparison complexity parameterized by the number n of input keys.
preconditions: The supplied algorithm has an O(n log n) comparison-count upper bound.
Derivation d1 applies to the same model and parameter.
fact_mapping: artifacts/facts.json states algorithm O(n log n).
scenario.md freezes the exact sequential comparison model with arbitrary keys and no preprocessing.
steps: The supplied algorithm gives an upper bound O(n log n).
Derivation d1 gives a model-wide lower bound Ω(n log n).
Because the bounds concern the same operation, parameter, and frozen model, the algorithm's bound is Θ(n log n).
A hypothetical exact o(n log n) comparison sorter would contradict the Ω(n log n) lower bound.
The optimization state is converged: the supplied asymptotic upper bound reaches the proven lower bound.
derivations.result: c1 is established: an O(n log n) exact sequential comparison sort is asymptotically optimal under the frozen model.
residual_mismatch: Asymptotic optimality does not establish constant-factor, space, stability, or implementation-performance superiority.

synthesis.selected_option: O(n log n) exact sequential comparison sort
basis: The decision-tree information bound requires Ω(n log n) comparisons.
The supplied algorithm achieves O(n log n), yielding Θ(n log n).
No o(n log n) exact algorithm exists without violating the frozen model.
concessions: The result does not rank algorithms sharing Θ(n log n) by constants, memory, stability, or practical runtime.
Faster asymptotics may be possible only under additional key structure or a non-comparison computational model.
